sections.label: USER PROMPT
sections.content:
The code executed 3 actions in the previous sequence.
Executed actions: LEFT, LEFT, UP.
You are still on the same level.
Current state: step 72, level 1.
Valid actions right now: UP, DOWN, LEFT, RIGHT, MOUSE.
Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`. It also provides `last_animation` (per-action frames of your last `action(...)` call) and `frame_stats` (how much this game has animated so far) -- optional; use them only if you judge the intermediate motion informative rather than incidental.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
Working world model carried from earlier turns:
- World model: UP didn't work - left green blocked by right dark outline above. Left green W at (30-32, 45-47), right dark outline at (26-30, 44-48). Need to find a way to get the left green inside the right dark outline.
- Plan: Try moving left green LEFT to go around the right dark outline, then UP, then RIGHT to enter from the side.
- Revise any item above immediately if `current_frame` or `history` contradicts it.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 1: you have used 71 actions; a strong score needs about 28 or fewer. You are 2.5x over the target.
If you are not steadily making progress toward the level goal, commit to your single best hypothesis and the shortest sequence that tests it — do not exhaustively scan rows/columns or enumerate every option. Test one idea, read the result, then decide.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
Interesting! After LEFT, the left green moved from (30-32, 45-47) to (30-32, 27-29). That's a big jump! It moved all the way to the corridor, not just one step left.
Then after LEFT, UP, RIGHT: the left green moved from (30-32, 27-29) to (30-32, 24-26). So LEFT moved it 3 columns left, UP didn't work (still at rows 30-32), and RIGHT moved it 3 columns right.
Wait, let me re-read:
After LEFT: left green at (30-32, 27-29), W center at (31, 28)
After LEFT, UP, RIGHT: left green at (30-32, 24-26), W center at (31, 25)
So LEFT moved from (30-32, 27-29) to (30-32, 24-26) (3 columns left). UP didn't work. RIGHT moved from (30-32, 24-26) to (30-32, 27-29) (3 columns right).
Wait, that doesn't make sense. Let me re-read:
After LEFT: left green at (30-32, 27-29)
After LEFT, UP, RIGHT: left green at (30-32, 24-26)
So the sequence was: LEFT (moved from 45-47 to 27-29), LEFT (moved from 27-29 to 24-26), UP (no change), RIGHT (moved from 24-26 to 27-29).
Wait, but the output says "After LEFT, UP, RIGHT" the left green is at (30-32, 24-26). So the sequence was:
1. LEFT: from (30-32, 45-47) to (30-32, 27-29)
2. LEFT: from (30-32, 27-29) to (30-32, 24-26)
3. UP: no change
4. RIGHT: from (30-32, 24-26) to (30-32, 27-29)
But the output shows the left green at (30-32, 24-26) after LEFT, UP, RIGHT. So RIGHT didn't work? Or UP moved it somehow?
Actually wait, let me look at the segmentation more carefully:
After LEFT, UP, RIGHT:
- color=N px=8 bound=[[30, 24], [30, 26], [32, 26], [32, 24]] - left green at (30-32, 24-26)
- color=W px=1 bound=[[31, 25]] - left green center at (31, 25)
So after LEFT, UP, RIGHT, the left green is at (30-32, 24-26). This means:
- LEFT: from (30-32, 45-47) to (30-32, 27-29)
- LEFT: from (30-32, 27-29) to (30-32, 24-26)
- UP: no change (still at 30-32, 24-26)
- RIGHT: no change (still at 30-32, 24-26)
Wait, but the actions were ['LEFT', 'UP', 'RIGHT']. So:
1. LEFT: from (30-32, 45-47) to (30-32, 27-29)
2. UP: no change
3. RIGHT: from (30-32, 27-29) to (30-32, 24-26)
That doesn't make sense either. RIGHT should move right, not left.
Hmm, I think the issue is that I'm confusing the two greens. Let me re-read:
After LEFT:
- color=N px=8 bound=[[30, 27], [30, 29], [32, 29], [32, 27]] - this is the left green (W center at 31, 28)
- color=N px=8 bound=[[30, 45], [30, 47], [32, 47], [32, 45]] - this is the right green (c center at 31, 46)
Wait, there are TWO N px=8 objects! One at (30-32, 27-29) with W center, and one at (30-32, 45-47) with c center. And there's also:
- color=w px=9 bound=[[30, 30], [30, 32], [32, 32], [32, 30]] - this is the white block
So after the first LEFT action, the left green moved from (30-32, 45-47) to (30-32, 27-29). But the right green is still at (30-32, 45-47). So the left green jumped from the right panel all the way to the corridor!
After LEFT, UP, RIGHT:
- color=N px=8 bound=[[30, 24], [30, 26], [32, 26], [32, 24]] - left green at (30-32, 24-26)
- color=N px=8 bound=[[30, 45], [30, 47], [32, 47], [32, 45]] - right green at (30-32, 45-47)
So after the sequence LEFT, UP, RIGHT, the left green moved from (30-32, 27-29) to (30-32, 24-26). The LEFT action moved it 3 columns left (from 27-29 to 24-26). UP didn't work. RIGHT didn't work (or maybe it did but was blocked?).
Wait, I think the issue is that the left green can only move LEFT/RIGHT in the corridor, not UP/DOWN. And when it's at (30-32, 24-26), it's in the corridor. Let me try moving it RIGHT to go back to the right panel.
Actually, let me reconsider. The left green is at (30-32, 24-26) in the corridor. The right panel is to the right of the purple bar. The purple bar is at approximately cols 33-38. So to get to the right panel, the left green needs to move RIGHT past the purple bar.
But earlier, when the left green was at the white block (30-32, 30-32), moving RIGHT took it to the right panel (30-32, 45-47). So the left green can pass through the purple bar when moving RIGHT.
Let me try: move the left green RIGHT to get back to the right panel, then try a different approach to absorb the right dark outline.
Actually, wait. I notice that the white block is now at (30-32, 30-32) with w color. And the right green is at (30-32, 45-47) with c center. So the right green moved from the white block to the right panel when the left green moved to the white block.
Hmm, this is getting confusing. Let me just focus on the current state and try to solve the level.
Current state:
- Left green: W center at (30-32, 24-26) in the corridor
- Right green: c center at (30-32, 45-47) in the right panel
- Right dark outline: c px=13 at (26-30, 44-48)
- Left dark outline: w px=9 at (33-35, 12-14)
The left green has W center and is in the corridor. I can move it RIGHT to the white block, then RIGHT to the right panel. But I already tried this and the left green ended up below the right dark outline, unable to move UP.
Let me think about this differently. The right dark outline is at (26-30, 44-48). The hollow center is at (27-29, 45-47). To absorb the right dark outline, the left green needs to be at (27-29, 45-47).
Currently, the left green is at (30-32, 24-26). If I move it RIGHT to the right panel, it will be at (30-32, 45-47), which is below the hollow center. I need to get it to (27-29, 45-47).
What if I move the left green UP when it's in the corridor? The corridor is at rows 30-32. If I move UP, the left green would go to rows 27-29. Then I can move RIGHT to the right panel, and it would be at (27-29, 45-47), which is the hollow center of the right dark outline!
Let me try: UP, RIGHT, RIGHT.

sections.label: ASSISTANT
sections.content:
World model: Left green W at (30-32, 24-26) in corridor. Right green c at (30-32, 45-47) in right panel. Right dark outline at (26-30, 44-48). Key insight: left green moves 3 cols per step in corridor. Need to move UP in corridor first (to rows 27-29), then RIGHT to enter right dark outline at its hollow center (27-29, 45-47).
Plan: Move UP in corridor, then RIGHT to right panel to absorb right dark outline.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Left green at (30-32, 24-26), try UP then RIGHT
result = action(['UP'])
seg = current_frame.segmentation
print("After UP:")
for n in seg['nodes']:
    if n['pixels'] <= 15:
        print(f"  color={n['color']} px={n['pixels']} bound={n['boundary']}")

if not result.get('level_completed'):
    result2 = action(['RIGHT'])
    seg2 = current_frame.segmentation
    print("\nAfter RIGHT:")
    for n in seg2['nodes']:
        if n['pixels'] <= 15:
            print(f"  color={n['color']} px={n['pixels']} bound={n['boundary']}")
    print("  level_completed:", result2.get('level_completed'))
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
